question: Which French author wrote "Les Misérables"?
funFact: Victor Hugo's Les Misérables contains one of the longest sentences in the French language — a single sentence of 823 words.

question: Albert Camus won the Nobel Prize in Literature in which year?
funFact: Camus, at 43, was the second-youngest laureate ever when he received the Nobel Prize in Literature.

question: Gustave Flaubert's 1857 novel "Madame Bovary" was initially prosecuted for what offence?
correctAnswers: Immorality and offence against public morals
funFact: Flaubert was acquitted, and the trial's publicity made Madame Bovary a bestseller.

question: Simone de Beauvoir's 1949 work "The Second Sex" is foundational to which movement?
funFact: The Second Sex was put on the Catholic Church's Index of Forbidden Books upon publication.

question: Which philosophical and literary movement, prominent in post-war France, was associated with Sartre and Camus?
funFact: Jean-Paul Sartre famously declined the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1964, the only person to voluntarily decline it.

question: Which Molière comedy centres on a miser so obsessed with his money that he drives away his own children?
funFact: Molière based The Miser on the ancient Roman comedy Aulularia by Plautus, yet set it in 17th-century Paris, making the greed feel timeless.

question: Victor Hugo's 1831 novel set in medieval Paris and centred on the Cathedral of Notre-Dame is commonly known in English as what?
funFact: Hugo wrote the novel partly to draw public attention to the neglected state of Gothic architecture in France; the book's popularity directly inspired restoration work on the real Notre-Dame cathedral.

question: Marcel Proust's seven-volume novel cycle "In Search of Lost Time" begins with which volume?
funFact: Swann's Way was initially rejected by several publishers, including Gallimard, so Proust paid for its publication himself in 1913 — Gallimard later signed him and published the rest of the cycle.

question: Émile Zola's 1898 open letter defending Alfred Dreyfus, published on the front page of a newspaper, is famous under what title?
funFact: Zola's letter was addressed directly to the French President and accused the army of anti-semitism and obstruction of justice; it resulted in Zola being convicted of libel and fleeing to England for nearly a year.

question: The French literary movement known as the Nouveau Roman (New Novel) rejected traditional narrative conventions. Which author is most closely identified as its founder?
funFact: Robbe-Grillet's 1957 manifesto "For a New Novel" argued that fiction should abandon psychology and plot in favour of precise, objective description of surfaces — an idea he called chosisme (thing-ism).

question: Stendhal's 1830 novel "The Red and the Black" takes its title from colours thought to represent what two career paths available to an ambitious young man in Restoration France?
funFact: Stendhal based his protagonist Julien Sorel on a real criminal case he read about in a newspaper; he wrote the entire novel in just 52 days.

question: Which French Romantic poet, known for collections such as "Les Fleurs du mal," was himself prosecuted for obscenity upon the work's 1857 publication?
funFact: Six poems were ordered removed from Les Fleurs du mal after the trial; they were not legally published in France until 1949 — nearly a century after Baudelaire wrote them.

question: In which newspaper was Zola's J'Accuse...! published?
funFact: L'Aurore was a liberal Paris daily. Zola's letter appeared on its front page on 13 January 1898.

question: To whom was Zola's open letter J'Accuse...! directly addressed?
funFact: Félix Faure was President of France at the time. Zola addressed the letter to him personally, accusing his government of antisemitism.

question: Alfred Dreyfus was convicted of what crime before Zola took up his cause?
funFact: Dreyfus was an officer on the French Army General Staff when he was convicted of passing military secrets. He was sentenced to lifelong penal servitude.

question: To which penal colony was Alfred Dreyfus exiled after his conviction?
funFact: Devil's Island is a small island off the coast of French Guiana. It was notorious as a harsh penal colony from which escape was nearly impossible.

question: What legal charge was Zola found guilty of following the publication of J'Accuse...!?
funFact: Zola was actually counting on the government suing him for libel — it was a strategic move to force weak evidence against Dreyfus into the open in court.

question: Which country did Zola flee to in order to avoid imprisonment after his libel conviction?
funFact: Zola lived in exile in England before returning to France in June 1899. He did not return until after the political climate surrounding the Dreyfus Affair had shifted.

question: On what date was J'Accuse...! published?
funFact: The letter appeared on the front page of L'Aurore on 13 January 1898. Zola was found guilty of libel just six weeks later, on 23 February 1898.

question: Who owned L'Aurore, the newspaper that published J'Accuse...!?
funFact: Georges Clemenceau was a prominent liberal politician who would later serve as Prime Minister of France. His newspaper L'Aurore was a natural home for Zola's provocative letter.

question: Beyond a jail sentence, what honour was stripped from Zola as part of his punishment for libel?
correctAnswers: His membership in the Legion of Honour
wrongAnswers: His seat in the Académie française
funFact: Zola was removed from the Legion of Honour, France's highest order of merit. His remains were later reinterred in the Panthéon in 1908, a posthumous restoration of his national standing.

question: How did Émile Zola die, four years after publishing J'Accuse...!?
correctAnswers: Carbon monoxide poisoning from a blocked chimney
funFact: Zola died at home from carbon monoxide poisoning. In 1953, a Parisian roofer confessed on his deathbed that he had deliberately blocked Zola's chimney to murder him.

question: When were Zola's remains transferred to the Panthéon in Paris?
funFact: Zola's remains were interred in the Panthéon six years after his death. The Panthéon is France's national mausoleum, reserved for citizens of the highest distinction.

question: In what year did a Parisian roofer confess to deliberately blocking Zola's chimney to murder him?
funFact: The confession came half a century after Zola's death in 1902. The roofer claimed he blocked the chimney intentionally, though the claim has never been officially verified.

question: What was the title of Bernard Lazare's 1896 pamphlet that also proclaimed Dreyfus's innocence?
correctAnswers: A Miscarriage of Justice: The Truth about the Dreyfus Affair
wrongAnswers: The Innocent Officer: A Defence of Dreyfus
funFact: Bernard Lazare's pamphlet predated Zola's famous letter by more than a year, published in November 1896. It was one of the earliest public defences of Dreyfus.

question: Zola's strategy in writing J'Accuse...! was deliberately provocative. What was his legal aim?
correctAnswers: To force the government to sue him for libel so weak evidence against Dreyfus would be exposed in court
wrongAnswers: To trigger a parliamentary inquiry into antisemitism
To compel Dreyfus's retrial through public pressure alone
To obtain an emergency presidential pardon for Dreyfus
To embarrass the army into admitting fault voluntarily
To bring the case before an international tribunal
To secure Dreyfus's release through a prisoner exchange
To provoke a constitutional crisis and new elections
funFact: Zola framed his accusations so broadly that prosecution became inevitable. A libel trial would require the government to produce the flimsy evidence used against Dreyfus, potentially unravelling the case.

question: In 1919, which French filmmaker released a film titled J'accuse as a statement against World War I?
funFact: Abel Gance's 1919 film J'accuse launched him to international fame. He later became renowned for his ambitious silent epic Napoléon (1927).

question: Which Palestinian Arab newspaper published a four-page editorial titled J'Accuse! protesting the Balfour Declaration?
funFact: Filastin, founded in 1911, was the most widely read Palestinian Arab newspaper in 1925. Its J'Accuse! editorial invoked Zola's letter to protest British support for Jewish settlement in Palestine.

question: Which German pacifist wrote a 1915 book titled J'Accuse! condemning the actions of the German Empire during World War I?
funFact: Richard Grelling's book was a remarkable act of dissent, criticising his own country's conduct in the war. It borrowed the moral force of Zola's letter to indict German militarism.

question: What role did Alfred Dreyfus hold in the French military when he was convicted of espionage?
correctAnswers: Officer on the French Army General Staff
wrongAnswers: Cavalry lieutenant in the colonial army
Military attaché to the German embassy
Infantry officer in the Foreign Legion
funFact: Dreyfus's position on the General Staff made his alleged espionage especially scandalous — he would have had access to sensitive strategic information.

question: An underground newspaper in Nazi-occupied France shared the name J'Accuse. Who edited it?
funFact: The name J'Accuse was deliberately chosen to evoke Zola's moral stand. Adam Rayski edited the paper as part of the Jewish resistance movement in occupied France.

question: When did Zola return to France after fleeing his libel conviction?
funFact: Zola spent nearly a year in exile in England before returning to France in June 1899. He had left immediately after his conviction to avoid imprisonment.
